mappings.rationaleAI: Directly addresses China's opium trade crisis, Commissioner Lin Zexu's confiscation provoking British military response, the Treaty of Nanking opening treaty ports and ceding Hong Kong (A.1.a, A.1.a.i), and the Second Opium War extending Western commercial privileges, which fueled anti-foreign sentiment concentrated at Canton (A.1.a.ii).

Hong Xiuquan's Taiping Heavenly Kingdom (1850-1864) is the central popular uprising covered under A.1.b, exposing Qing administrative weakness, empowering regional military commanders, and causing an estimated twenty million deaths that reshaped China's political trajectory.

The 1900 Boxer uprising and siege of foreign legations in Peking directly corresponds to A.2.c (anti-foreign feelings, Western seizure of Peking, further Ch'ing concessions). The rebellion's role in catalyzing political reform movements leading to the 1911 revolution connects to A.2.d.

Cixi dominated the Chinese court for nearly fifty years as regent ruling through child emperors, embodying the tension between westernization and tradition (A.2). Her contested relationship with modernizing reforms (A.2.a), role during the Boxer crisis (A.2.c), and debates over whether she obstructed or advanced late Qing reform (A.2.d) make her central to the entire self-strengthening and reform narrative.

Covers the Tokugawa isolation policies and Commodore Perry's 1853-54 forced reopening of Japan, which are the immediate preconditions for the fall of the Tokugawa shogunate and the Meiji Restoration described in B.1.a.

Japan's 1904-05 victory over Russia demonstrated that rapid Meiji-era modernization (B.1.b) could produce a military power capable of defeating a European empire, confirming Japan's emergence as a world power (B) and shocking Western assumptions about Asian capability.

Korea's brief assertion of sovereignty (1897-1910) and its annexation by Japan in 1910 illustrate the geopolitical consequences of the Sino-Japanese War and foreign encroachments in East Asia (A.2.b explicitly mentions Korea), while Korea's fate under Japanese imperialism reflects Japan's emergence as a world power (B).
